coredata.dc:title: Anticancer potential of Bellardia trixago quantum dots: Cytotoxic effects on various cancer cell lines

Sarı, Zeynep Betul
Sarı, Muhammet Emin
Durmaz, Alper
Torunoğlu, Emine Incilay
Gümrükçüoğlu, Abidin
Demirel, Gamze
coredata.dc:description: This study investigates the synthesis, characterization, and anticancer effects of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from Bellardia trixago. The CQDs were analyzed using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). TEM results revealed that the CQDs have a spherical morphology and exhibit a layered structure. XRD analysis showed a graphite-like crystalline structure, while FTIR and XPS studies confirmed the presence of OH, CC, and CO functional groups on the surface. The biological activity of CQDs demonstrated selective cytotoxicity, inducing significant cell death in cancer cells while exhibiting low toxicity in healthy cells. More pronounced morphological changes were observed in HEp-2 and SaOS-2 cells, while HEK-293 cells showed negligible changes. These findings suggest that quantum dots could serve as a potential alternative for cancer treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bellardia trixago
Carbon quantum dots
Cytotoxicity
Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy
Transmission Electron microscopy
X-ray diffraction
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological changes in cancer and healthy cells following quantum dot treatment
Thermal synthesis of quantum dots from B. Traxigo
Assessment of cell toxicity using the MTT assay

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the as-prepared BT-CQDs using of B. trixago.
XRD images of the as-prepared BT-CQDs using of B. trixago.
FTIR spectra of the as-prepared BT-CQDs using of B. trixago.
XPS images of the as-prepared BT-CQDs using of B. trixago.
UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs, b: the image of CQDs under daylight (right) and under UV-light at 365 nm (left).

In vitro cytotoxic activity % (mean ± SD) of quantum dots against (B) MCF-7, (C) HEC1B, (D) Hep-2, (E) PANC-1, (F) A-549, (G) SaOS-2, 24-h post-treatment as compared with (A) HEK-293 a normal cell line. Statistical significance is indicated as follows: *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001. ns = not significant.

Dose-Response Curves of quantum dots in various human cancer cell lines (MCF-7, HEC1B, Hep-2, PANC-1, A-549, and SaOS-2) and the HEK-293 healthy cell line after 24-h treatment, assessed using the MTT assay. The x-axis represents the quantum dot concentrations (0.1 mg/mL, 5 mg/mL, and 50 mg/mL), while the y-axis indicates the relative cell viability (%) compared to the control (100 %).

Microscopic images of various cancer cell lines (A) (PANC-1, A549, HEp2, SaOS2, HEC1B, and MCF-7) and (B) the HEK-293 healthy cell line, at 10× magnification using an inverted light microscope. Cells were observed before (control) and after 24-h treatment with 5 mg/mL of quantum dots, illustrating the effects on cell viability and morphology under these conditions. Comparing to untreated cells, (A) In treated PANC1 and A549 cells reduced cell density, shrinkage, and detachment indicate moderate to significant cytotoxicity; in treated HEp2 and Saos2 cells increased detachment and rounding suggest higher sensitivity to CQDs; in treated HEC-1-B and MCF7 cells marked reduction in cell density, membrane shrinkage, and apoptotic features confirm strong cytotoxic effects. (B) In treated healthy cell line (HEK293) minimal morphological changes, slight confluency reduction was observed.
Cell viability percentages after 24-h treatment with 5 mg/mL of quantum dots in various cancer cell lines and the HEK-293 healthy cell line.

head.ce:title: Anticancer potential of Bellardia trixago quantum dots: Cytotoxic effects on various cancer cell lines

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates the synthesis, characterization, and anticancer effects of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from Bellardia trixago. The CQDs were analyzed using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). TEM results revealed that the CQDs have a spherical morphology and exhibit a layered structure. XRD analysis showed a graphite-like crystalline structure, while FTIR and XPS studies confirmed the presence of OH, CC, and CO functional groups on the surface. The biological activity of CQDs demonstrated selective cytotoxicity, inducing significant cell death in cancer cells while exhibiting low toxicity in healthy cells. More pronounced morphological changes were observed in HEp-2 and SaOS-2 cells, while HEK-293 cells showed negligible changes. These findings suggest that quantum dots could serve as a potential alternative for cancer treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data generated or analyzed during this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are defined as fluorescent nanomaterials with sizes smaller than 10 nm [1]. Available in crystalline or amorphous forms, CQDs exhibit a lattice constant of 3.4 Å, corresponding to the interlayer spacing of graphite [2]. The natural color of carbon is black, and CQDs possess distinct characteristics such as strong fluorescence, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, high stability, excellent conductivity, and environmental friendliness. These nanoparticles typically exhibit optical absorption in the ultraviolet (UV) region, while also displaying an absorption tail extending into the visible wavelength range. The fluorescent properties of CQDs stand out as one of their most remarkable qualities, relevant for both fundamental research and applied studies [3].

Nature offers widely available and easily accessible resources as alternatives to chemical reagents, presenting nearly infinite possibilities. One such resource is the synthesis of nanomaterials from natural biomass, a topic that has been the focus of various studies and offers a broad range of applications [4]. Carbon dots derived from plants have numerous advantages, including low toxicity, biocompatibility, and renewable sources. Additionally, they possess excellent water solubility and high stability, making them easily synthesized using simple, low-cost methods [5]. Nanoparticles obtained through green biosynthesis emerge as innovative tools for cancer diagnosis as prognostic biomarkers and for drug delivery to tumor cells. These nanoparticles can exert effects by either damaging tumor cells or protecting healthy ones; this impact occurs through mechanisms involving the unique properties of the nanoparticles and the antioxidant and antitumor characteristics found in plants [6].

Green biomass-based synthesis offers an environmentally friendly approach by eliminating the need for expensive and toxic chemicals, while also reducing energy consumption. This method holds significant potential as an alternative pathway for synthesizing stable and biocompatible CQDs. One of the traditional methods commonly used for synthesizing CQDs from biomass through thermal conversion is pyrolysis. Pyrolysis stands out as a simple and effective method for preparing green CQDs. In this process, carbon precursors are heated to synthesize CQDs, followed by degradation and purification steps. Pyrolysis allows for the carburation of precursors at varying pressures and temperatures. Physical and chemical changes occur because of the irreversible thermal decomposition reactions of organic compounds in an inert environment, leading to the formation of solid residues with high carbon content [2].

Many commercially available organic fluorophores, despite exhibiting high emission quantum yields, come with disadvantages such as limited biodegradability and toxicity. In contrast, emerging CQDs possess superior features, including low cytotoxicity, high aqueous stability, and high emission quantum yield [7]. These characteristics make carbon CQDs a promising next-generation carbon nanomaterial for biomedical applications, utilized in biosensors and drug delivery for cancer and life-threatening health issues [8].

Previous studies have highlighted that the methanol extract of S. maritima exhibits strong antioxidant and antimicrobial activities, as emphasized by Aytar, who also notes that ST-CQDs possess similar antioxidant properties. Li et al. successfully demonstrated that carbon dots prepared from fresh tender ginger juice significantly suppressed HepG2 growth while exhibiting low toxicity when compared to normal liver cells (FL83B) and mammary epithelial cells (MCF-10 A) [9]. Xie et al. reported that carbon dots synthesized from date seeds showed strong inhibition activity against human breast cancer (MCF-7), denocarcinomic human alveolar basal epithelial cells (A-549), and human prostate cancer (PC-3) cells [10]. Arkan et al. found that carbon dots synthesized from walnut oil exhibited effective cytotoxic activity against PC-3, MCF-7, and human colorectal adenocarcinoma cell line (HT-29) [11]. Additionally, Marković et al. observed the highest photo-cytotoxic activity against RD cells derived from human rhabdomyosarcoma in the case of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) at 5.35 μg/mL, which was approximately 3.8 times higher compared to cis-diamminedichloroplatinum (Cis-DDP).The lowest photo-cytotoxic activity was detected in the graphene oxide example, which exhibited the highest activity specifically against the murine fibroblast cells (L2OB) (32.50 μg/mL). The quantum dots (GQDs) example demonstrated the highest photo-cytotoxic activity against the RD cells (20.94 μg/mL). The nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) example showed the highest photo-cytotoxic activity against the HEp-2 cells derived from human cervical carcinoma [12].

The discovery of new potential therapies plays a crucial role in biomedical research. In this context, in vitro studies are essential for evaluating the efficacy and safety of treatment options. In vitro research involves the use of cell cultures and biological models to preliminarily assess the biological interactions, pharmacokinetic properties, and therapeutic potential of novel compounds. These studies offer significant advantages in terms of time and cost, providing controlled and rapid results before transitioning to animal models. Additionally, in vitro experiments provide fundamental insights into identifying therapeutic targets, conducting toxicity tests, and understanding mechanism of actions. Thus, in vitro research is an indispensable tool in the development of new therapeutic strategies and in the transition to clinical applications [13].

The aim of the study is to characterize CQDs synthesized from Bellardia trixago and evaluate their cytotoxic effects on human cancer cells. The morphological, crystalline structure, chemical bonding, and optical properties of the synthesized CQDs were analyzed using TEM, XRD, FTIR, and XPS techniques. Additionally, the cytotoxic effects of these nanomaterials were determined using the MTT assay on various cancer cell lines (MCF-7, HEC1B, HEp-2, PANC-1, A-549, SaOS-2) and compared with healthy HEK-293 cells. The primary objective of the study is to investigate the selective cytotoxicity potential of CQDs and assess their suitability as biocompatible and effective nanomaterials for future cancer treatment applications.

The results of the TEM analysis conducted to examine the crystal structure of the synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are presented in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1a shows the general morphological distribution of the CQDs, where numerous spherical particles exhibit a relatively homogeneous distribution with some regions showing aggregation tendencies. The nanoparticles are clearly visible in the image. Upon examining Fig. 1b, it was observed that the particles have a layered structure, and these layers stack on top of each other to form a complex morphology.

The crystal properties of the carbon quantum dots were characterized by XRD measurements, and the results are shown in Fig. 2 . The sharp peaks observed in the chromatogram confirm that the material has a crystalline structure. The characteristic peak observed at 25.261° corresponds to the (002) plane in graphene structures. The broad and high-intensity nature of this peak indicates that the obtained carbon nanoparticles possess a graphite-like structure. Additionally, peaks observed at 37.701°, 48.020°, and 62.618° correspond to the (100), (101), and (112) planes, respectively, confirming the hexagonal crystal structure of the carbon nanoparticles [14,15]. The sharp peaks observed at other angles further support the crystalline structure.

FTIR spectroscopy was employed to elucidate the chemical bonding structure of the CQDs, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . Broad peaks associated with OH stretching vibrations were observed around 3300 cm−1. The peak at 2920 cm−1 corresponds to CH stretching vibrations, while the peaks at 1556 cm−1 correspond to CC stretching vibrations. The peaks observed at 1360 cm−1 correspond to bending vibrations of CH or OH bonds, and the peaks around 1020 cm−1 correspond to CO stretching vibrations. These findings confirm the presence of OH functional groups on the surface of the graphite-like CQDs.

To further investigate the types of chemical bonds in the structure, C1s and O1s XPS analyses were performed for the most abundant carbon and oxygen elements. As shown in Fig. 4a, the main peak in the 284–286 eV range indicates the presence of CC, CC, and CO bonds in the graphene layers of the CQDs. The peak observed in the 530–532 eV range in Fig. 4b confirms the presence of OH groups, etheric CO bonds, and CO groups on the surface of the CQDs. These results are in full agreement with the data obtained from the FTIR spectrum [16].

When examining the optical properties, as shown in Fig. 5 , the CQDs exhibit broad absorption bands extending from the UV region to the visible region. This observation is consistent with CQD results reported in the literature [17]. The observed broad absorption bands are due to π → π* electronic transitions between carbon atoms, confirming the graphite-like structure. Fig. 5b presents the behavior of the CQD solution under different lighting conditions. The solution, which appears yellow under normal room light (right), exhibits blue emission when exposed to UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm (left). This type of blue emission is a characteristic feature of CQDs in the literature. The wavelength of the emitted light can vary depending on the synthesis method used and the type of starting carbon source. The multicolored emission exhibited by the CQDs is indicative of their characteristic photoluminescent properties [18–20].

In the research, the cytotoxicity levels of the quantum dots were assessed in human cancer cells using the MTT test. Cell viability percentages after 24-h treatment with 5 mg/mL of quantum dots in various cancer cell lines and the HEK-293 healthy cell line were presented (Table 1 ). The 5 mg/mL concentration was specifically chosen for the table to facilitate a direct comparison between the cytotoxic effects on cancerous and healthy cells at a relevant dose level. At 5 mg/mL, HEK293 cells exhibited a cell viability of 97.5 %, indicating minimal cytotoxicity in healthy cells. This is crucial in demonstrating the selective cytotoxicity of CQDs, as their impact on cancer cells is more pronounced while sparing healthy cells. The viability percentages for cancer cell lines at 5 mg/mL (ranging from 77.5 % to 89.25 %) illustrate a clear reduction compared to HEK293 cells. This dose-dependent effect reinforces the hypothesis that CQDs may selectively affect cancer cells while maintaining high biocompatibility with normal cells. This concentration represents an intermediate dose where the contrast between cancerous and healthy cells is evident, without reaching excessive cytotoxicity that could obscure selectivity effects.

In the HEK-293 healthy cell line, the 0.1 mg/mL and 5 mg/mL doses did not exhibit significant toxic effects, with cell viability maintaining close to 100 %. However, the 50 mg/mL dose reduced cell viability to approximately 60 %, indicating toxicity at higher concentrations. These results suggest that quantum dots are non-toxic to healthy cells at lower doses but can induce toxicity at higher concentrations (Fig. 6A). In MCF-7 cells, a slight reduction in viability was observed at the 0,1 mg/mL dose (p < 0.05). The most pronounced decrease in cell viability occurred at the 5 mg/mL dose (p < 0.0001), while the 50 mg/mL dose also resulted in a significant reduction, though less effective compared to 5 mg/mL (p < 0.01). These findings indicate that quantum dots exhibit dose-dependent cytotoxicity in MCF-7 cells, with the strongest effect observed at the 5 mg/mL concentration (Fig. 6B). Quantum dots were ineffective at the low dose (0.1 mg/mL) in the HEC-1B cell line; however, a significant reduction in cell viability was observed at the 5 mg/mL and 50 mg/mL doses. These results indicate that quantum dots exhibit cytotoxic effects on HEC-1B cells at higher concentrations (Fig. 6C). In the HEp-2 cell line, quantum dot treatment resulted in a significant reduction in cell viability at 5 mg/mL (p < 0.001), while the 0.1 mg/mL and 50 mg/mL doses did not produce statistically significant effects (Fig. 6D). Quantum dot treatment resulted in a significant reduction in cell viability in PANC-1 cells at all tested concentrations. At 0.1 mg/mL, cell viability was significantly reduced (p < 0.01), while the 5 mg/mL dose showed the strongest reduction (p < 0.001). The 50 mg/mL dose also led to a notable decrease in viability (p < 0.01), indicating that quantum dots have a dose-dependent cytotoxic effect on PANC-1 cells (Fig. 6E). In the A-549 cancer cell line, quantum dots exhibited significant cytotoxic effects, particularly at 5 mg/mL and 50 mg/mL doses. However, the 0.1 mg/mL dose did not demonstrate a significant impact on cell viability (Fig. 6F). Quantum dots exhibited no significant effects at low and medium doses in the SaOS-2; however, a strong and significant cytotoxic effect was observed at the 50 mg/mL dose (Fig. 4G). The dose-response relationships of quantum dots in different cell lines are further illustrated in Fig. 7 highlighting the variation in cytotoxic effects across doses and cell types.

This study primarily focuses on evaluating the cytotoxic effects of CQDs derived from Bellardia trixago using the MTT assay. While our findings suggest selective cytotoxicity towards cancer cells, we acknowledge that this study does not explore the molecular mechanisms underlying this effect, such as apoptotic pathways or oxidative stress responses. Investigating apoptosis requires specialized assays, including Annexin V/PI staining, TUNEL assay, caspase activity assays, and Western blot analysis, which were beyond the current scope due to infrastructure and resource constraints. However, we recognize the importance of understanding the precise mechanisms of CQD-induced cytotoxicity and plan to conduct further mechanistic studies in future research. These will include reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation and apoptotic signaling pathways, providing a deeper insight into CQD-mediated cell death mechanisms. Despite these limitations, our findings contribute to the growing body of research on CQDs as potential anticancer agents, offering a foundation for future investigations into their molecular interactions in biological systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological changes in cancer and healthy cells following quantum dot treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Microscopic examination of the cells, captured at 10× magnification using an inverted light microscope, reveals notable differences in cell morphology between the control and quantum dot-treated groups across various cancer cell lines. In the control group (untreated), the cells in all cancer lines, including PANC-1, A-549, HEp-2, SaOS2, HEC1B, and MCF-7 appear healthy, with a typical adherent morphology and intact cell membranes. The cells exhibit a dense distribution with clear cellular boundaries and minimal signs of stress or apoptosis. Following treatment with 5 mg/mL of quantum dots for 24 h, the cancer cells demonstrate morphological changes indicative of cytotoxicity. PANC-1 and A-549 cells show slight cellular shrinkage and reduced confluency, suggesting moderate cytotoxic effects. HEp-2 and SaOS2 cells have more pronounced changes, with increased cellular detachment and altered cell shape, suggesting higher sensitivity to quantum dots. HEC-1B and MCF-7 cells have significant reduction in cell density and visible signs of apoptosis, with rounded and detached cells being more evident, indicating stronger cytotoxic effects in these cell lines (Fig. 8A).

In contrast, the HEK-293 healthy cells display minimal morphological changes after quantum dot treatment, with only a slight reduction in confluency and no significant signs of apoptosis or cellular damage (Fig. 8B). This suggests that quantum dots exhibit selective cytotoxicity, affecting cancer cells more than healthy cells.

When collectively evaluating the data from all cancer cell lines and healthy cell lines, the 5 mg/mL dose was toxic to many cancer cells while causing no serious harm to healthy cells. Thus, 5 mg/mL could be a suitable and safe starting dose for cancer treatment. The 50 mg/mL dose demonstrated the highest cytotoxicity in cancer cells but should be used with caution due to its toxic effects on healthy cells.

It was observed that HEp-2 and SaOS-2 cell lines were ineffective at low doses, with a notable effect seen only at 50 mg/mL in SaOS-2 cells, suggesting it may be less effective. A-549 cells were particularly sensitive to treatment with quantum dots at 5 mg/mL and 50 mg/mL. In PANC-1 cells, a clear cytotoxic effect was noted, which may be promising for treatment-resistant cancers like pancreatic cancer. In HEC-1B cells, significant cytotoxic effects were observed at medium and high doses, indicating that it may serve as a good target for treatment.

The 5 mg/mL dose exhibits cytotoxic effects in several cancer cell lines while causing minimal harm to healthy cells. Therefore, 5 mg/mL could be considered a suitable and safe starting dose for cancer treatment. In contrast, the 50 mg/mL dose induces the highest level of cytotoxicity in cancer cells but also demonstrates toxicity in healthy cells, warranting cautious use.

The data indicates that quantum dots exhibit moderate cytotoxicity across a variety of cancer cell lines, with the greatest impact observed in MCF-7, Hep-2, and HEC-1B cells. SaOS-2 cells are relatively resistant, while PANC-1 and A-549 cells show only a slight reduction in viability. Importantly, HEK-293 healthy cells are largely unaffected by the treatment, suggesting that quantum dots may offer a therapeutic advantage by targeting cancer cells while sparing normal, healthy cells. To improve therapeutic outcomes, higher doses or combination treatments might be explored to increase cytotoxicity in resistant cancer types. Overall, quantum dots show promise as a potential therapeutic agent.

The observed morphological changes in the cancer cells following quantum dot treatment are consistent with the MTT assay results, which also demonstrated significant reductions in cell viability across most cancer cell lines. The decrease in cell density, cellular shrinkage, and detachment in the treated groups further corroborate the cytotoxic effects detected by the MTT assay. These morphological indicators, such as cell rounding and loss of adherence, are hallmark signs of apoptosis and cell death, aligning with the decreased viability observed quantitatively in the MTT data. This congruence between morphological evidence and cell viability assays reinforces the conclusion that quantum dots effectively induce cytotoxicity in cancer cells, while sparing the healthy HEK-293 cell line, further supporting the potential of quantum dots as a selective therapeutic agent.

In the study by Kumar et al. [21], CQDs synthesized from Anisomeles indica were reported to inhibit cell viability by 50 % in triple-negative breast cancer cell lines (TNBC, MDA-MB-231), with an IC50 value of 3.22 ± 0.64 μg/mL. Additionally, the CQDs synthesized from A. indica were found to be non-toxic to HEK-293 cells. Kumar et al. highlighted the potent cytotoxic effects of bright blue CQDs synthesized from A. indica on TNBC cell lines (MDA-MB-231). In contrast, in our study, quantum dots at 0.1 mg/mL and 5 mg/mL concentrations did not induce significant toxicity in healthy HEK-293 cells, with cell viability remaining close to 100 %. However, at a concentration of 50 mg/mL, cell viability dropped to approximately 60 %, indicating that while the quantum dots are non-toxic to healthy cells at lower doses, they exhibit toxicity at higher concentrations. In MCF-7 cells, dose-dependent cytotoxicity was observed, with a slight reduction in cell viability at 0.1 mg/mL, and the most pronounced decrease occurring at 5 mg/mL. Interestingly, at 50 mg/mL, a reduction in viability was also noted, though the effect was less than that observed at 5 mg/mL. Similarly, Kumar et al. reported cytotoxic effects in breast cancer cells, with both studies demonstrating strong cytotoxicity of quantum dots in breast cancer cells, though higher concentrations were required in our study.

In the study by Kumar et al. [22], CQDs synthesized via microwave-assisted green synthesis from Mesosphaerum suaveolens exhibited an IC50 value of 0.77 μg/mL against the A-549 cancer cell line. In our study, significant cytotoxic effects were observed at 5 mg/mL and 50 mg/mL concentrations, while the 0.1 mg/mL dose did not exhibit significant effects. Kumar et al. reported notable anticancer activity of carbon quantum dots against A-549 cells.

In the study by Ali et al. [23], CQDs synthesized via green biosynthesis from watermelon rind waste were found to have an IC50 value of 114 μg/mL in the MCF-7 cell line, with inhibition rates of 99 % and 93 % at concentrations of 1000 μg/mL and 500 μg/mL, respectively. The results indicated that CQDs synthesized from watermelon rind waste exhibited strong anticancer activity. Our findings also showed that quantum dots exhibited significant cytotoxic effects, particularly at higher concentrations (5 mg/mL and 50 mg/mL), with the strongest effect observed at 5 mg/mL in MCF-7 cells. Ali et al. reported that CQDs synthesized from watermelon rind exhibited near-complete inhibition of cell viability at high concentrations (1000 μg/mL), showing potent anticancer effects. Their findings, along with other studies, support the use of quantum dots and CQDs as potential anticancer agents. However, the study by Ali et al. noted that CQDs from watermelon rind displayed stronger anticancer effects in MCF-7 cells.

In the study by Malavika et al. [24], CQDs synthesized from Aloe barbadensis using a microwave-assisted reflux method exhibited an IC50 value of 52.2 ± 1.35 μg/mL in the MCF-7 breast cancer cell line, indicating significant antiproliferative effects on human breast cancer cells. Both Malavika et al.'s study and our findings show that CQDs exert cytotoxic effects on cancer cells. However, the IC50 values and concentrations required to induce toxicity vary significantly depending on the type of nanoparticle and cell line used. Our findings demonstrated that quantum dots exhibit strong dose-dependent cytotoxicity in various cancer cell lines, particularly at a concentration of 5 mg/mL, while Malavika et al. observed a marked reduction in cell viability in MCF-7 cells.

In the study by Naik et al. [25], the IC50 value of 2.0 mg/mL for CQDs synthesized from Andrographis paniculata using a hydrothermal method in the MCF-7 cancer cell line, with a significant dose-dependent reduction in cell viability. The concentration of quantum dots required to induce significant cytotoxicity in MCF-7 cells (5 mg/mL) in our study was higher than the IC50 value (2.0 mg/mL) reported for CQDs. While our study emphasizes the pronounced cytotoxicity of quantum dots at higher concentrations (5 mg/mL), Naik et al. demonstrated the effective cytotoxicity of CQDs in MCF-7 cells.

In the study by Arkan et al. [11], CQDs synthesized via green synthesis from Juglans regia significantly reduced cell viability in PC3, MCF-7, and head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC) cell lines, with IC50 values of 0.00125 mg/mL, 0.005 mg/mL, and 0.01 mg/mL, respectively. In our study, a 5 % reduction in cell viability was observed at a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL, while a 30 % reduction was observed at 5 mg/mL. Compared to Arkan et al.'s study, the quantum dots in our study exhibited cytotoxic effects at higher concentrations.

In the study by Shivaji et al. [26], the IC50 value of 14 μg/mL for CQDs synthesized via green synthesis from Camellia sinensis in the A-549 cancer cell line, demonstrating significant cytotoxicity. In our study, quantum dots exhibited significant cytotoxicity at concentrations of 5 mg/mL and 50 mg/mL in A-549 cells. Both studies reported cytotoxic effects in A-549 cells, though Shivaji et al. demonstrated efficacy at much lower concentrations.

In the study by Li et al. [9], CQDs synthesized from ginger juice exhibited an IC50 value of 0.35 mg/mL in the HEp-2 cell line, indicating significant cytotoxic potential against liver cancer cells. Our study also demonstrated that quantum dots exhibited dose-dependent cytotoxicity in various cancer cell lines, particularly at higher concentrations. The cytotoxic potential of quantum dots has been confirmed in both studies; however, mechanisms and cellular responses may vary depending on the specific cell line and type of nanoparticle used.

In recent years, the production and development of carbon dots have rapidly advanced, and their potential for use in various fields, such as therapeutic and diagnostic agents, sensors, and photocatalysts, has increased [27,28]. Although they have been found to exhibit low toxicity due to their high stability, biocompatibility, and eco-friendly properties, the metabolism and elimination pathways are not yet fully understood [29]. While the use of carbon dots as drug carriers in cancer treatment has become widespread, some carbon dots can exhibit cytotoxic properties, potentially posing a risk to healthy cells due to the increased reactive oxygen species [30–32]. The data obtained in our study show that CQDs exhibit cytotoxic effects in certain cancer cell lines. However, the exact mechanisms through which this effect occurs have not been fully elucidated. The literature suggests that CQDs may increase cellular toxicity through oxidative stress, cause mitochondrial dysfunction, or activate apoptotic signaling pathways. For instance, it has been shown that CQDs increase ROS production, leading to cytotoxic effects in MCF-7 cells [33]. Similarly, CQDs produced from walnut oil have been found to exhibit anticancer effects against MCF-7 and PC-3 cells (approximately IC50 of 1.25 ± 0.062 μg/cc, 5 ± 1.03 μg/cc, and > 10 μg/cc, respectively). The induction of apoptosis by CQDs has occurred alongside an increase in caspase-3 activation [34]. Methanol extracts of walnut leaves, green husk, and seeds have been found to inhibit cell growth against kidney carcinoma cell lines (A498 and 769P) and Caco-2 cells [35]. Juglans regia leaf extract has been shown to inhibit the growth of PC-3 cells through apoptosis (IC50 = 48.4 μg/mL) [36]. Camellia sinensis-based CQDs have been found to exhibit cytotoxic effects against A-549 cancer cells. Furthermore, CdS QDs have been shown to arrest A-549 cell growth in the S phase of the cell cycle, inhibiting the proliferation of lung cancer cells [37]. CdS quantum dots obtained from green cardamom fruit extracts (CdS-Cm QDs) have been shown to exhibit significant cytotoxic effects on the MCF-7 cancer cell line, inhibiting cell growth at low concentrations. Evaluations using lactate dehydrogenase and apoptosis assays showed an IC50 value of 27.3 μg/mL, indicating that CdS-Cm QDs have an effective cytotoxic potential against cancer cells [38]. Distinguishing apoptotic and necrotic cell death pathways, analyzing changes in mitochondrial potential, and evaluating the expression levels of pro-apoptotic proteins (e.g., Bax, Caspase-3) will provide a more comprehensive understanding of the cellular toxicity of CQDs.

The cytotoxicity evaluation was conducted solely using the MTT assay, and neither molecular mechanisms nor specific apoptotic pathways were examined. The synthesized B. traxigo quantum dots exhibited cytotoxic effects in MCF-7, HEC1B, HEp-2, PANC-1, A-549, and SaOS-2 cancer cell lines, while minimal toxicity was observed in the HEK-293 healthy cell line at lower doses. Higher doses, however, led to more pronounced toxicity in cancer cells. Microscopic examinations revealed significant morphological changes in cancer cells treated with quantum dots, while only minimal changes were observed in HEK-293 healthy cells. These findings suggest that quantum dots may serve as a potential alternative for cancer treatment. However, further research on the mechanisms of action is needed to draw definitive conclusions. However, further research on the underlying mechanisms is needed to reach definitive conclusions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal synthesis of quantum dots from B. Traxigo
ce:section.ce:para: The pyrolysis method was selected for the synthesis of B. traxigo. To carry out this process, 2 g of the sample were heated at 200 °C for 1 h. Subsequently, 20 mL of distilled water was added to the sample, and the RL formed were extracted for 30 min. The extraction solution was then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was diluted with distilled water at a 1:20 ratio, and absorption spectra in the range of 200–1000 nm were recorded using quartz cuvettes, with distilled water serving as the blank [2].

Carbon quantum dots (B. trixago) were prepared by dissolving 1 g of the material in 10 ml of distilled water. The solution was mixed thoroughly and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h to ensure complete dissolution. Following incubation, the supernatant was carefully filtered using a sterile filter to remove any particulates and ensure sterility. This preparation yielded a 100 mg/mL stock solution. For experimental use, the stock solution was diluted in culture media to achieve final working concentrations of 0.1 mg/mL, 5 mg/mL, and 50 mg/mL [2].

The CQDs synthesized from B. trixago were analyzed using various advanced techniques to understand their physical, chemical, and structural properties. The optical properties of the CQDs were examined using ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy. Absorption spectra were recorded with an Agilent UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA). This analysis helped determine the electronic transitions and bandgap energy of the CQDs, which are important for their potential applications in optoelectronics.

The elemental composition and structure of the CQDs were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and X-ray diffraction (XRD). XPS was performed with an ESCALAB MK II spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) to identify the chemical bonds on the CQD surface. XRD analysis was conducted using a Bruker D2 diffractometer (Bruker Corporation, Billerica, MA, USA) with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) to examine the crystal structure. These analyses provided important information about the atomic arrangement and chemical properties of the B. trixago-based CQDs.

The internal structure and size of the CQDs were further studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). TEM images were taken with an FEI TALOS F200S TEM (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) at 200 kV. For this analysis, the CQD solution was diluted with ethanol and carefully dropped onto carbon-coated copper grids, then left to dry at room temperature. This ensured a proper dispersion of the CQDs, making it easier to observe individual particles.

The chemical properties and surface functional groups of the CQDs were identified using Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The FTIR spectra were recorded with a Shimadzu IR Prestige-21 spectrometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Kyoto, Japan) to determine the functional groups present in the lyophilized CQD samples. This analysis provided key insights into the chemical structure of the CQDs and the influence of Bellardia trixago in their synthesis.

The cancer cell lines used in this study included PANC-1 (pancreatic carcinoma), A-549 (adenocarcinomic human alveolar basal epithelial cells), HEp-2 (human laryngeal squamous cell carcinoma), SaOS-2 (human osteosarcoma), HEC-1B (human endometrial carcinoma), and MCF-7 (human breast cancer). HEK-293 cells (human embryonic kidney) were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC) and served as a healthy control cell line. All cells were maintained in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) (Gibco, USA) supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) (Gibco, USA) and 1 % penicillin/streptomycin (Gibco, USA) in a humidified 5 % CO2 incubator at 37 °C. After reaching 80–90 % confluence, the cells were detached using trypsin-EDTA (Sigma-Aldrich) and plated in 96-well plates (1 × 10^4 cells/well) for experiments. The prepared quantum dot solutions were applied to the cell cultures at final concentrations of 0.1 mg/mL, 5 mg/mL, and 50 mg/mL. Nontreated cells served as the control group. After treatment, the cells were incubated with quantum dots for 24 h under the same culture conditions (37 °C, 5 % CO₂). The 24-h incubation period for quantum dot (CQD) treatment was selected based on previous studies demonstrating that this duration is optimal for assessing early cytotoxic effects and apoptotic responses in cancer cell lines [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of cell toxicity using the MTT assay
ce:section.ce:para:
The impact of quantum dots on cell viability was assessed using the biochemical in vitro technique known as MTT (4,5-dimethyl diphenyl tetrazolium bromide). The MTT assay was carried out according to the previously mentioned protocol (insert citation to prior study). In summary, cells were cultured for 24 h following quantum dot therapy. The plates were incubated for three hours at 37 °C after 20 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well. After carefully removing the medium, DMSO was applied to each well to dissolve the formazan crystals. The MTT assay was performed in two independent experiments, each conducted with seven replicates (n = 7 per group). A microplate reader was used to measure absorbance at 570 nm, and the following formula was used to calculate the percentage of cell viability:
Cell viability
%
=
OD
of sample
OD
of control
× 100

The results from the MTT assay were utilized to calculate the IC50 values for quantum dots. Absorbance data were entered into an IC50 calculation tool, which generated IC50 values for each concentration of quantum dots. These values were determined for each cell line to assess the cytotoxic effects of the quantum dots. However, the IC50 value could not be calculated from the data, as cell viability did not reach 50 % inhibition at any of the tested doses (0.1 mg/mL, 5 mg/mL, and 50 mg/mL). IC50 refers to the concentration that reduces cell viability by 50 % compared to the control group. Specifically, even at the highest concentration of 50 mg/mL, cell viability remained around 82–84 %. Therefore, additional tests at higher doses are required to determine the IC50. Nonetheless, in this context, using quantum dot doses beyond those reported in the literature is not advisable. Consequently, the percentage viability at the medium dose was calculated and included in the table.

The mean value ± SD is used to display the data. At least three separate runs of the study were carried out. GraphPad Prism 9 (GraphPad Software, Inc., La Jolla, CA, USA) was used to create the graphs. With GraphPad Prism software, an unpaired t-test was used for statistical analysis. Statistical significance was defined as a p-value of less than 0.05. Statistical significance is indicated as follows: *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001. ns = not significant.
There is no financial support.

Erdi Can Aytar: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Zeynep Betul Sarı: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Muhammet Emin Sarı: Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Alper Durmaz: Resources, Investigation. Emine Incilay Torunoğlu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Resources, Investigation. Abidin Gümrükçüoğlu: Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Gamze Demirel: Resources, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bioactive compounds of Plantago arenaria and their effects on SH-SY5Y cells: phenolic content and docking analysis
Chem. Afr.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.B. Sarı, M.E. Sarı, E.I. Torunoğlu, G. Demirel, B. Aydın, E.C. Aytar, Bioactive Compounds of Plantago arenaria and Their Effects on SH-SY5Y Cells: Phenolic Content and Docking Analysis, Chemistry Africa (2024). doi:10.1007/S42250-024-01093-Z.
